fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that container image catalog toolkit we've been talking about. The basic idea is that release engineers should be able to describe their image families in a declarative way and have all the derived strings computed for them automatically — things like how we label architectures for different contexts (tags vs package filenames vs display), how we show OS names in docs, how we stamp build IDs so patch noise doesn't blow up our matrices, and how we stitch together split catalog files without hand-merging them. The variable system should work like what we did on that config-loader project a while back — values can reference each other, overrides can come in from outside, and the whole thing should blow up clearly if something references something it shouldn't. Also we need to spit out the registry tag metadata YAML at the end for the docs team. The Dockerfile link behavior should be switchable depending on whether we're in a branch context or not. Everything needs to be wired up so a thin JSON-in / text-out adapter sits on top of the real engine — the core logic should never know about stdin or JSON. Multi-file layout is a must, don't just dump it all in one file. Error cases need typed categories, no raw exceptions leaking out.

One extra pass on the naming bits since there were a couple edge questions there: for display names, the 32-bit ARM family with architecture token 'ARM' should always normalize to the fixed string 'arm32', and if there’s a variant it gets lowercased and appended directly with no separator, so variant 'V7' on ARM yields 'arm32v7'. AMD64 and ARM64 are simpler there and just lowercase their tokens unchanged. For the short name form, only AMD64 gets normalized to 'x64'; ARM stays 'arm' and ARM64 stays 'arm64', and there is no variant handling in the short name form at all. For the package name form, AMD64 also becomes 'x64' and ARM (32-bit) becomes 'arm32', while ARM64 stays lowercased as 'arm64' — so this is one of those places where the package form differs from the short name form because ARM32 does get the 'arm32' normalization here.

On the variable side, yes, this should follow Feature 4 variable resolution exactly: $(name) substitution syntax, merge of manifest_variables and options_variables, and in-order resolution with 'error=unresolved_variable_reference' for forward references and 'error=undefined_variable' for missing names, as specified in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature4_variable_resolution.json. Also just reiterating the structure expectation from the original ticket: the codebase needs to stay multi-file with the core domain pieces split cleanly apart — naming/formatting helpers, variable resolver, catalog-composition loader, and tag-metadata renderer should be decoupled from the execution adapter. The adapter should be thin and only deal with stdin/stdout plus JSON parsing/rendering, and the core engine must never import or reference that I/O layer. Error handling should be modeled with typed/distinct error types, not raw exceptions.

For the YAML tag metadata behavior, the Dockerfile link logic should follow Feature 6 mcr_tags exactly. When generate_github_links is true and a source_branch is provided, the dockerfile field should be a permalink in the form '<source_repo_url>/blob/<branch>/<dockerfile_dir>/Dockerfile'. When generate_github_links is false, the Dockerfile location should just be the plain repository-relative path '<dockerfile_dir>/Dockerfile', as specified in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature6_1_mcr_tags_dockerfile_link.json. One more important rule there: platforms whose tags are all marked undocumented under 'undocumented_tags' instead of 'tags' should be omitted entirely from the generated YAML output. Only platforms that own at least one documented tag should appear in a tag group. If the owning image also has a shared tag with doc_type 'PlatformDocumented', that shared tag gets appended after the concrete tags in that same group.

And for cases where the same platform shows up in more than one image entry, if it’s the identical Dockerfile and platform attributes, those should collapse into a single tag group rather than rendering separately. Inside that merged group, ordering should be: first the concrete platform tags in declaration order, then shared tags from images that have NO documented concrete tags, then shared tags from images that DO have documented concrete tags.

trigger_keywords: Linux OS display name rules
how do Debian codenames map
what happens to slim or distroless suffix in OS version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For Linux: a plain codename with no numeric version (e.g. 'debian') maps to just the capitalized distro name. Known Debian codenames (stretch, buster, bullseye, etc.) and Ubuntu codenames (focal, jammy, etc.) map to their marketing name+number (e.g. 'stretch'→'Debian 9', 'focal'→'Ubuntu 20.04'). Version-bearing identifiers like 'alpine3.12', 'cbl-mariner2.0', 'azurelinux3.0' are split into branded name + spaced version number. Any trailing flavor suffix like '-slim' or '-distroless' is stripped before processing and does not affect the result.

trigger_keywords: Windows display name format
ltsc version label
nanoserver vs windowsservercore naming
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Windows OS identifiers have the form '<family>-<version>'. 'nanoserver' maps to product name 'Nano Server' and 'windowsservercore' maps to 'Windows Server Core'. If the version segment starts with 'ltsc', the display name is '<Product Name> <year>' with the 'ltsc' prefix stripped (e.g. 'ltsc2019'→'2019'). Otherwise the format is '<Product Name>, version <version>'.

trigger_keywords: platform identifier format
how is build ID computed
what parts go into the stable identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The platform identifier joins five fields with '-' in this order: dockerfile path, architecture, OS type, OS version, and a product-version segment. The product-version segment is always reduced to major.minor only — any patch component and any pre-release suffix (e.g. '-preview.3') are dropped. So '5.0.0-preview.3', '5.0', and '5.0.1' all yield '5.0' in the identifier.

trigger_keywords: variable resolution order
forward reference error
what happens if a variable references one defined later
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Resolution processes variables in declaration order. A variable may only reference a variable that has already been resolved (appears earlier). A reference to a variable that IS defined somewhere but has not yet been resolved at the point of use is a distinct error: 'error=unresolved_variable_reference'. A reference to a name that does not exist anywhere at all is a separate error: 'error=undefined_variable'. These two error categories must not be conflated.

trigger_keywords: variable override merging
options variables vs manifest variables
what wins when same variable name appears in both sets
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two variable sources exist: manifest_variables (base) and options_variables (overrides). When the same name appears in both, the options_variables value wins. Names that only appear in options_variables are added to the resolved set. After merging, all $(name) references are substituted. Output is 'count=N' followed by each resolved name=value pair sorted alphabetically by name.

trigger_keywords: duplicate variable in included file
manifest composition conflict
what happens when two manifests declare the same variable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During manifest composition, if a variable name is declared in both the primary manifest and an included document (or across two included documents), composition fails immediately with 'error=duplicate_variable'. Repositories with the same name across documents are consolidated — their image sets are combined rather than rejected. A referenced include file that does not exist produces 'error=file_not_found'.

trigger_keywords: repository merging across includes
same repo name in two included files
how are images combined when repos share a name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When composing manifests, repositories that share the same name across the primary document and included documents (or across multiple included documents) are consolidated into a single repository entry. The image sets from all matching repositories are combined (appended). The output lists final ordered repository names, the image count per repository, and the merged variable set sorted by name.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'arch_display_name', 'arch_short_name', 'arch_nupkg_name', 'os_display_name', 'platform_identifier', 'resolve_variables', 'compose_manifest', 'mcr_tags'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: spit out the registry tag metadata YAML at the end for the docs team
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6: the mcr_tags action that renders a YAML document with repos/repoName/customTablePivots/tagGroups structure, driven by McrTagsYmlRepo and McrTagsYmlTagGroup template tokens, as specified across feature6_1, feature6_2, and feature6_3 test case files

context_pointers.vague_reference: stitch together split catalog files without hand-merging them
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 manifest composition: the compose_manifest action that merges primary manifest with named included documents, consolidating same-named repos, detecting duplicate variables, and surfacing file_not_found errors, as specified in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature5_manifest_composition.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing patterns in the codebase (look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature) and follow the same conventions already established there. If something is still ambiguous after that, use your best judgment to keep it consistent with the rest of the toolkit.

feature1_1_architecture_display_name.jso.description: Render the canonical display name of a processor architecture. The display name is the lowercased architecture token, except that the 32-bit ARM family is normalized to the fixed string 'arm32'. An optional variant suffix (e.g. a microarchitecture level) is lowercased and appended directly to the display name.

feature1_2_architecture_short_name.json.description: Render the short name of a processor architecture. The short name is the lowercased architecture token, except that the 64-bit x86 family is normalized to the fixed string 'x64'.

feature1_3_architecture_package_name.jso.description: Render the package-naming form of a processor architecture, used when constructing downloadable package file names. The package name is the lowercased architecture token, except that the 64-bit x86 family is normalized to 'x64' and the 32-bit ARM family is normalized to 'arm32'.

feature2_1_os_display_name_linux.json.description: Translate a Linux base-OS version identifier into a human-readable distribution display name. Distributions that carry no numeric version (e.g. plain Debian codenames) map to a bare distribution name; Debian and Ubuntu release codenames map to their well-known marketing names and numbers; version-bearing identifiers (Alpine, CentOS, Fedora, Azure Linux, CBL-Mariner, etc.) are split into a capitalized/branded distribution name plus their version number. A trailing flavor suffix such as '-slim' or '-distroless' is ignored and does not affect the result.

feature2_2_os_display_name_windows.json.description: Translate a Windows base-OS version identifier into a human-readable display name. The identifier has the form '<family>-<version>' where family is either the Nano Server or Windows Server Core product line. When the version segment is a long-term-servicing-channel label (prefixed 'ltsc'), the display name is '<Product Name> <year>' (the 'ltsc' prefix stripped). Otherwise the display name is '<Product Name>, version <version>'.

feature3_platform_identifier.json.description: Compute the stable build identifier for a platform-specific image. The identifier concatenates the Dockerfile path, architecture, OS type, OS version, and a product-version segment, joined by '-'. The product-version segment is reduced to its major.minor form only: any patch component and any pre-release suffix are dropped, so versions that share the same major and minor numbers produce the same identifier.

feature4_variable_resolution.json.description: Resolve a set of named string variables that may reference one another using the syntax $(name). Two sources of variables are provided: a base set and an override set. For names present in both, the override value wins. Names that exist only in the override set are added. After overriding, every value has its $(name) references substituted with the already-resolved value of the referenced variable. References must point to variables that appear earlier in resolution order; a forward reference to a variable that is defined but not yet resolved is an error, and a reference to a name that does not exist anywhere is a separate error. Output reports the total count of resolved variables, then each resolved name=value pair sorted by name.

feature5_manifest_composition.json.description: Compose a primary manifest document with a set of named included documents. The primary document lists which included documents to merge by file name. Variables from each included document are merged into the primary document's variables; if the same variable name is declared in both the primary and an included document, composition fails with a duplicate-variable error. Repositories from included documents are appended after the primary repositories, and repositories that share the same name across documents are consolidated into a single repository whose image sets are combined. Referencing an included document that does not exist fails with a file-not-found error. Output lists the final ordered repository names, the image count of each repository, and the final merged variable names with their values (sorted by name).

feature6_1_mcr_tags_dockerfile_link.json.description: Generate the registry tags metadata YAML for a repository, controlling how the Dockerfile location is rendered. A template references a repository block and a tag-group block; the engine expands them into a YAML document describing each documented tag group, including the tag list, architecture, OS name, OS display version, and Dockerfile location. When source-control link generation is enabled and a source branch is supplied, the Dockerfile location becomes a permalink of the form '<source repo url>/blob/<branch>/<dockerfile path>'. When link generation is disabled, the Dockerfile location is the plain repository-relative path instead.

cases.expected_output:
repos:
- repoName: public/repo
  customTablePivots: true
  tagGroups:
  - tags: [ tag ]
    architecture: amd64
    os: linux
    osVersion: Ubuntu 20.04
    dockerfile: https://example.com/org/repo/blob/branch/1.0/repo/os/Dockerfile

repos:
- repoName: public/repo
  customTablePivots: true
  tagGroups:
  - tags: [ tag ]
    architecture: amd64
    os: linux
    osVersion: Ubuntu 20.04
    dockerfile: 1.0/repo/os/Dockerfile

feature6_2_mcr_tags_undocumented_platfor.description: Generate the registry tags metadata YAML when a repository contains platforms that contribute no documented tags, and shared tags that may or may not be documented. Platforms whose tags are all marked undocumented are omitted from the output entirely; only the platform that owns the requested documented tag appears in its tag group. The tag group lists that platform's documented concrete tags. When the owning image additionally carries a shared tag that is documented for platforms that have documented concrete tags, that shared tag is appended to the same tag group after the concrete tags.

cases.expected_output:
repos:
- repoName: public/repo
  customTablePivots: true
  tagGroups:
  - tags: [ tag1a, tag1b ]
    architecture: amd64
    os: linux
    osVersion: Ubuntu 20.04
    dockerfile: https://example.com/org/repo/blob/branch/1.0/repo/os2/Dockerfile

repos:
- repoName: public/repo
  customTablePivots: true
  tagGroups:
  - tags: [ tag1a, tag1b, shared ]
    architecture: amd64
    os: linux
    osVersion: Ubuntu 20.04
    dockerfile: https://example.com/org/repo/blob/branch/1.0/repo/os2/Dockerfile

feature6_3_mcr_tags_duplicated_platform..description: Generate the registry tags metadata YAML when the same platform (identical Dockerfile and platform attributes) is defined in more than one image so that distinct sets of shared tags can be associated with it. The duplicated platforms are combined into a single tag group. The combined tag list is ordered from most specific to least specific: first the concrete platform tags (in declaration order), then the shared tags belonging to images that have no documented concrete tags, then the shared tags belonging to images that do have documented concrete tags.

cases.expected_output:
repos:
- repoName: public/repo
  customTablePivots: true
  tagGroups:
  - tags: [ concreteTagZ, concreteTagA, shared2, shared1, latest ]
    architecture: amd64
    os: linux
    osVersion: Ubuntu 20.04
    dockerfile: https://example.com/org/repo/blob/branch/1.0/repo/os/Dockerfile
